headline: How Netflix uses all 7 S3 storage classes to store its entire content library
why: Netflix has 36,000+ titles. A trending show today might be forgotten in 2 years. Storing everything in Standard would cost tens of millions extra per year. Netflix maps every title to the right storage class based on viewing patterns.

topics.usage: Currently trending shows (Stranger Things S5, new releases) — accessed millions of times per day. Stored in Standard for instant delivery to CDN.
Shows from 2–3 years ago with moderate viewership. Still available on the platform, accessed weekly. Standard-IA saves ~40% on storage vs Standard.
Content licensed but rarely watched — old documentaries, regional content with low global demand. Netflix can restore in 3–5 hours if a title suddenly goes viral (e.g. a featured news story).
Original camera footage, uncut masters, content whose license expired. Must be kept for legal/archival reasons. May never be accessed again. Costs pennies per GB per month.

New international content with unknown demand (Korean, Turkish, Nigerian shows). Netflix doesn't know if it will trend globally or stay niche — Intelligent Tiering lets AWS figure it out automatically.

diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph HOT["🔥 Hot — Standard"]
        T1[Trending Shows\nMillions of daily plays]
    end
    subgraph WARM["🌤️ Warm — Standard-IA"]
        T2[2–3 Year Old Content\nWeekly plays]
    end
    subgraph COLD["❄️ Cold — Glacier FR"]
        T3[Rarely Watched\nRestore in hours]
    end
    subgraph ARCHIVE["🗄️ Archive — Deep Archive"]
        T4[Raw Masters / Expired\n12–48hr restore]
    end
    subgraph AI["🤖 Unknown — Intelligent Tiering"]
        T5[New International\nAI decides tier]
    end
    CDN[CloudFront CDN] --> HOT
    CDN -->|on demand| WARM
    WARM -->|lifecycle| COLD
    COLD -->|lifecycle| ARCHIVE
    style HOT fill:#7c2d12,color:#fed7aa
    style WARM fill:#1e3a5f,color:#dbeafe
    style COLD fill:#1a3320,color:#86efac
    style ARCHIVE fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8
    style AI fill:#4a1d96,color:#e9d5ff
takeaway: Netflix doesn't pick one storage class — they use all seven, mapped to actual viewing data. The result: the same 11-nines durability for every title, but drastically different costs depending on how hot the content is. This is S3 storage classes in production.
